ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for noodle soup with fried pork chop; closest_parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent category; no specific code exists for noodle soup with fried pork chop in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of noodle soup with fried pork chop is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of wheat noodles in pork bone broth topped with one deep-fried pork chop cutlet.

nutrient_source.title: Noodle soup with pork — composite dish estimation based on HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for pork noodle soup and fried pork components
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exactly matches Din Tai Fung's noodle soup with fried pork chop. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo data for pork noodle soup (broth + wheat noodles component) with USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried pork chop (USDA FDC ID 167903, pork loin chop, cooked, pan-fried). Per-100g values are derived from a modelled 550 g serving: approximately 300 g noodle-broth component and 250 g fried pork chop component. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and noodle-based dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Instant noodles, boiled' and 'Wheat noodles, boiled' entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for noodle soup with fried pork chop. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat noodles (GI range 47–58, mean ~52). The fried pork chop component contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (52 × 74.25 g carbohydrate in 550 g serving) / 100 ≈ 38.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork bone broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (≈2310 mg per 550 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming less soup liquid can substantially lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the wheat noodle component contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 38.6); portion control, choosing a smaller noodle portion, or substituting with lower-GI noodles such as soba or shirataki can help manage postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried pork chop and sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate intake of this dish due to its saturated fat content from the deep-fried pork chop (estimated 1.9 g saturated fat per 100 g) and high sodium from the broth; consuming the dish occasionally and limiting broth intake can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Noodle Soup with Fried Pork Chop (排骨面, Pái Gǔ Miàn) is a Chinese-style soup noodle dish consisting of wheat noodles served in a rich pork bone broth, topped with a deep-fried pork chop cutlet. The dish is a signature item at Din Tai Fung, the internationally acclaimed Taiwanese restaurant chain with outlets across Singapore and globally. The pork chop is typically marinated, coated in a light batter or breadcrumb crust, and deep-fried to achieve a crispy exterior while remaining tender and juicy inside. The broth is slow-cooked from pork bones and seasoned with soy sauce, giving it a savoury, umami-rich flavour. The noodles are smooth and springy wheat noodles. The dish is garnished with spring onions and sometimes bok choy. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from the pork chop (~9.8 g/100 g), moderate fat (~6.2 g/100 g) from the frying process, and carbohydrates (~13.5 g/100 g) primarily from the noodles. Sodium content is notably high due to the broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low GI), though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate-to-high. Clinically, portion control and reduced broth consumption are advisable for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Din Tai Fung's noodle soup with fried pork chop exists in HPB Foodo or USDA databases. Values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo pork noodle soup data with USDA SR Legacy fried pork chop data, modelled for a 550 g serving. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data used for this composite estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this dish type. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried pork loin data and are approximate. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. Actual nutrient values at Din Tai Fung may differ from these estimates due to proprietary recipes and preparation methods. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodle entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific dish was found. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 74.25) / 100 ≈ 38.6 for a 550 g serving containing approximately 74.25 g available carbohydrate.
